articles.title: Power MOSFET Selection Guide
articles.shortDescription: Comprehensive guide for selecting IXYS power MOSFETs for your application
articles.content: Selecting the right power MOSFET is critical for power electronics design success. This guide provides a systematic approach to MOSFET selection based on application requirements, electrical specifications, and thermal considerations.

sections.heading: Understanding Voltage Rating Requirements
sections.content: The voltage rating of a MOSFET must accommodate the maximum drain-source voltage with adequate safety margin. For 380V AC input systems, the DC bus voltage is approximately 540V, requiring a 600V or higher MOSFET. Always include a 20-30% safety margin above the maximum expected drain voltage to account for voltage transients and switching spikes.

sections.heading: Current Rating Selection
sections.content: Current rating selection involves calculating the RMS drain current under worst-case operating conditions. For switching applications, consider the continuous current, peak current during transients, and thermal derating at high ambient temperatures. The MOSFET's current rating should exceed the calculated RMS current by at least 20% to ensure reliable operation.

sections.heading: Rds(on) and Conduction Losses
sections.content: On-resistance (Rds(on)) is a critical parameter determining conduction losses. Lower Rds(on) reduces conduction losses but typically comes with higher gate charge and cost. Calculate conduction loss as Pcond = I² × Rds(on) × duty cycle. Consider the temperature coefficient of Rds(on) which increases approximately 0.5% per degree Celsius above 25°C.

faqs.question: How do I determine the required voltage rating for my MOSFET?
faqs.answer: To determine voltage rating: (1) Calculate maximum drain voltage: For bridge circuits, Vds(max) = Vdc bus. (2) Add safety margin: 20-30% above maximum expected voltage. (3) Consider transients: Add margin for voltage spikes from switching. (4) Temperature derating: Voltage rating decreases at high temperature. (5) Example: 380V AC PFC → 540V DC → 700V with margin → Select 800V or 1000V MOSFET. (6) Standard ratings: 500V for 220V AC, 600-800V for 380V AC systems. Always use next standard voltage rating above calculated requirement.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 600V MOSFETs for 380V AC systems with adequate margin; 500V for 220V AC.

faqs.question: What is the trade-off between Rds(on) and gate charge?
faqs.answer: Rds(on) vs gate charge trade-off: (1) Low Rds(on) - Reduces conduction losses but increases die size. (2) Gate charge - Larger die has higher Qg, increasing drive losses. (3) Optimization - Balance conduction and switching losses for your frequency. (4) High frequency (>100kHz) - Prioritize low Qg over Rds(on). (5) Low frequency (<50kHz) - Prioritize low Rds(on). (6) Figure of merit - Qg × Rds(on) for comparison. (7) Cost - Lower Rds(on) typically costs more. Optimize for total losses at operating conditions.
faqs.decisionGuide: For high frequency, prioritize low Qg; for low frequency, prioritize low Rds(on).

faqs.question: How do I calculate total power losses in a MOSFET?
faqs.answer: Total power loss calculation: (1) Conduction loss: Pcond = Irms² × Rds(on) × duty cycle. (2) Switching loss: Psw = (Eon + Eoff) × fsw. (3) Gate drive loss: Pgate = Qg × Vgs × fsw. (4) Output capacitance loss: Pcoss = 0.5 × Coss × Vds² × fsw. (5) Example at 20A, 100kHz: Pcond = 400 × 0.085 × 0.5 = 17W, Psw = 50W, Total = 67W. (6) Use datasheet values at operating temperature. (7) Include temperature effects on Rds(on). Total losses determine thermal management requirements.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate all loss components; design thermal management for worst-case conditions.

faqs.question: What gate drive voltage should I use for IXYS MOSFETs?
faqs.answer: Gate drive voltage recommendations: (1) Standard drive: +10V to +12V for most applications. (2) Logic level: +5V for logic-level MOSFETs. (3) Threshold voltage: Must exceed Vgs(th) for full conduction. (4) Gate charge: Higher Vgs reduces Rds(on) slightly. (5) Negative voltage: Not required for MOSFETs (unlike IGBTs). (6) Maximum rating: Never exceed ±30V (or datasheet limit). (7) Drive power: P = Qg × Vgs × fsw. Use +12V for standard MOSFETs; +5V only for logic-level devices.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use +12V gate drive for standard MOSFETs; ensure adequate drive current capability.

faqs.question: How do I select the gate resistor value?
faqs.answer: Gate resistor selection: (1) Purpose - Controls switching speed and EMI. (2) Lower resistance - Faster switching, higher EMI. (3) Higher resistance - Slower switching, lower EMI, reduced dv/dt. (4) Typical values - 5Ω to 50Ω depending on application. (5) Power rating - P = Qg² × fsw × Rg (usually small). (6) Switching loss trade-off - Optimize for total losses. (7) EMI requirements - May need higher Rg for compliance. Start with 10Ω and adjust based on waveforms and EMI testing.
faqs.decisionGuide: Start with 10Ω; increase for EMI reduction, decrease for lower switching loss.

faqs.question: Can I parallel MOSFETs for higher current capability?
faqs.answer: Paralleling MOSFETs guidelines: (1) Current sharing - Positive tempco of Rds(on) helps balance current. (2) Gate drive - Use common driver with individual gate resistors (1-5Ω). (3) Layout - Symmetrical layout with equal trace lengths critical. (4) Current imbalance - Expect 10-20% variation. (5) Derating - Use 80% of combined current rating. (6) Testing - Verify current sharing with current probes. (7) Applications - Common for high-current supplies. With proper design, paralleled MOSFETs can achieve 2-4x individual current capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use individual gate resistors; implement symmetric layout; verify current sharing.

faeInsights.author: FAE Team
faeInsights.content: This article provides valuable technical information.

articles.title: IGBT Selection and Application Guide
articles.shortDescription: Complete guide for selecting and applying IXYS IGBTs in power electronics
articles.content: IXYS IGBTs are widely used in motor drives, inverters, and power supply applications. This guide covers IGBT selection criteria, gate drive requirements, protection strategies, and thermal management for reliable operation.

sections.heading: IGBT vs MOSFET Selection
sections.content: Choose IGBT for applications above 300V and below 100kHz switching frequency where conduction losses dominate. Choose MOSFET for lower voltage or higher frequency applications where switching losses dominate. IGBTs offer lower conduction losses at high voltage but have higher switching losses and switching times compared to MOSFETs.

sections.heading: Voltage and Current Ratings
sections.content: Select voltage rating with 20-30% margin above maximum operating voltage. For 380V AC systems, use 600V IGBTs; for 690V systems, use 1200V IGBTs. Current rating should exceed RMS current by at least 20%, considering overload requirements and thermal derating.

sections.heading: Switching Characteristics
sections.content: IGBT switching performance is characterized by turn-on delay, rise time, turn-off delay, and fall time. Switching losses increase with frequency and must be balanced against conduction losses. Faster switching reduces switching losses but may increase EMI and voltage overshoots.

faqs.question: When should I choose IGBT over MOSFET?
faqs.answer: IGBT vs MOSFET selection criteria: (1) Voltage - IGBT preferred above 300V. (2) Frequency - IGBT for <100kHz, MOSFET for >100kHz. (3) Conduction loss - IGBT lower at high voltage/current. (4) Switching loss - MOSFET lower at high frequency. (5) Cost - IGBT often lower cost per amp at high voltage. (6) Ruggedness - IGBT typically more rugged for short-circuit. (7) Applications - IGBT for motor drives, MOSFET for SMPS. Choose IGBT for high-voltage, moderate-frequency applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use IGBT for 300V+ and <100kHz; MOSFET for lower voltage or higher frequency.

faqs.question: What is Vce(sat) and why is it important?
faqs.answer: Vce(sat) explained: (1) Definition - Collector-emitter saturation voltage when IGBT is on. (2) Typical values - 1.5V to 3V depending on device and current. (3) Conduction loss - Pcond = Vce(sat) × Ic × duty cycle. (4) Temperature - Vce(sat) increases with temperature. (5) Comparison - Lower is better for efficiency. (6) Trench vs planar - Trench IGBTs have lower Vce(sat). (7) Trade-off - Lower Vce(sat) may mean slower switching. IXGH40N60B3D1 has 1.9V typical Vce(sat).
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose lowest Vce(sat) for conduction-loss-dominated applications; balance with switching speed.

faqs.question: How do I protect IGBTs from short-circuit conditions?
faqs.answer: IGBT short-circuit protection: (1) Detection method - Desaturation detection (monitoring Vce during on-state). (2) Response time - Must shutdown within 10μs (IXYS rating). (3) Soft turn-off - Gradual turn-off to limit voltage overshoot. (4) Gate drive - Use gate drivers with built-in desat protection. (5) Current limiting - May use current sensors as backup. (6) Testing - Verify protection under actual fault conditions. (7) Coordination - Ensure faster than fuse clearing. Fast protection is essential for IGBT survival.
faqs.decisionGuide: Implement desaturation detection with <5μs response; use soft turn-off.

faqs.question: What is the recommended gate drive for IXYS IGBTs?
faqs.answer: IGBT gate drive requirements: (1) Gate voltage - +15V for turn-on, -5V to -8V for turn-off. (2) Negative voltage - Prevents false turn-on from dv/dt. (3) Gate resistor - 10-47Ω typical, controls switching speed. (4) Drive power - P = Qg × (Vge+ + |Vge-|) × fsw. (5) Isolation - 2500Vrms minimum for 600V IGBTs. (6) Protection - Desaturation detection essential. (7) Miller clamp - Helps prevent false turn-on. Use isolated gate drivers with comprehensive protection features.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use +15V/-8V gate drive with desaturation protection; select Rg for EMI vs loss trade-off.

faqs.question: How does temperature affect IGBT performance?
faqs.answer: Temperature effects on IGBTs: (1) Vce(sat) - Increases with temperature (positive tempco). (2) Switching speed - Generally slows at high temperature. (3) Switching losses - May increase slightly at high temperature. (4) Thermal runaway - Positive tempco prevents thermal runaway. (5) Current rating - Must derate above 25°C case. (6) Reliability - Reduced at high junction temperatures. (7) Design - Keep Tj below 125°C for long life. Temperature derating is essential for reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Design for Tj < 125°C; account for increased Vce(sat) at high temperature.

faqs.question: What is the maximum switching frequency for IGBTs?
faqs.answer: IGBT switching frequency limitations: (1) Practical maximum - 50-100kHz depending on device. (2) Recommended range - 5-20kHz for motor drives. (3) Loss trade-off - Higher frequency increases switching losses significantly. (4) At 50kHz - Switching losses may dominate. (5) Thermal limits - Check total losses vs thermal capability. (6) Applications - Motor drives typically 5-16kHz. (7) MOSFET alternative - Consider MOSFET for >50kHz. Higher frequencies enable smaller filters but reduce efficiency.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use 5-20kHz for motor drives; verify thermal performance at chosen frequency.

faeInsights.content: This article provides valuable technical information.

articles.title: Thyristor and Rectifier Selection Guide
articles.shortDescription: Guide for selecting IXYS thyristors and rectifiers for power control applications
articles.content: IXYS thyristors (SCRs) and rectifiers are essential components for AC power control and rectification. This guide covers selection criteria, triggering requirements, protection strategies, and application considerations for reliable operation.

sections.heading: Thyristor Types and Applications
sections.content: IXYS offers phase-control thyristors for AC power control, fast switching thyristors for high-frequency applications, and standard SCRs for general-purpose use. Phase-control thyristors are optimized for line-frequency applications like motor soft starters and heating controls. Fast thyristors are designed for inverter and chopper circuits operating at higher frequencies.

sections.heading: Voltage and Current Ratings
sections.content: Select voltage rating with 30-50% margin above peak operating voltage to account for line transients. Current rating must handle RMS current under worst-case conditions, including overload requirements. Consider surge current rating for applications with high inrush currents like motor starting or capacitor charging.

sections.heading: Gate Trigger Requirements
sections.content: Thyristors require adequate gate trigger current and voltage to turn on reliably. Gate trigger current (Igt) and voltage (Vgt) specifications must be met under all operating conditions including low temperature where trigger requirements are highest. Use optocouplers or pulse transformers for isolation between control and power circuits.

faqs.question: What is the difference between SCR and triac?
faqs.answer: SCR vs triac comparison: (1) Conduction - SCR conducts in one direction only; triac conducts in both directions. (2) Applications - SCR for DC control and half-wave AC; triac for full-wave AC phase control. (3) Quadrants - Triac operates in all four quadrants; SCR in one quadrant. (4) Sensitivity - SCR generally more sensitive gate trigger. (5) dv/dt rating - Triac typically lower dv/dt rating. (6) Commutation - Triac has commutation limitations at inductive loads. (7) Selection - Use SCR for high-power DC or half-wave; triac for AC lamp/heater control.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use SCR for DC or high-power half-wave; triac for full-wave AC phase control.

faqs.question: How do I size a thyristor for motor soft starter applications?
faqs.answer: Thyristor sizing for motor soft starters: (1) Motor current - Size for motor full-load current. (2) Overload - Include 150% overload capability. (3) Surge current - Must handle 6-8x motor inrush current. (4) Voltage rating - 2-3 × line voltage for safety margin. (5) Example: 30kW, 380V motor → 57A FLC → Select 95A SCR with 1600V rating. (6) Three-phase - Use three SCRs (or pairs) for three-phase. (7) I²t rating - Coordinate with protection fuses. Proper sizing ensures reliable motor starting.
faqs.decisionGuide: Size for motor FLC with overload margin; verify surge current capability for inrush.

faqs.question: What is dv/dt and why is it important for thyristors?
faqs.answer: dv/dt considerations for thyristors: (1) Definition - Rate of voltage rise across device. (2) Critical dv/dt - Maximum rate before false triggering. (3) Static dv/dt - When device is off. (4) Commutating dv/dt - During turn-off (lower rating). (5) False triggering - High dv/dt can turn on SCR unintentionally. (6) Snubber circuit - RC network limits dv/dt. (7) Design - Always use snubber for inductive loads. The dv/dt rating ensures reliable operation in switching applications.
faqs.decisionGuide: Always use RC snubber for inductive loads; calculate values based on circuit inductance.

faqs.question: How do I protect thyristors from overcurrent?
faqs.answer: Thyristor overcurrent protection: (1) Semiconductor fuses - Fast-acting fuses with I²t coordination. (2) I²t coordination - Fuse I²t must be less than device I²t. (3) Current limiting - Series reactor or resistor. (4) Overcurrent relay - Fast-acting protection. (5) Gate protection - Prevent gate overvoltage. (6) Thermal protection - Heatsink overtemperature switch. (7) Surge protection - MOVs for voltage transients. Proper protection is essential as thyristors have limited surge capability.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use semiconductor fuses with proper I²t coordination; implement fast overcurrent detection.

faqs.question: What is the difference between standard and fast recovery rectifiers?
faqs.answer: Standard vs fast recovery comparison: (1) Recovery time - Standard: 1-10μs, Fast: 25-100ns. (2) Applications - Standard for line frequency rectification, Fast for high-frequency switching. (3) Switching loss - Fast diodes have much lower switching loss. (4) Forward voltage - Standard often has lower Vf. (5) Cost - Fast diodes typically 2-3x more expensive. (6) EMI - Fast diodes may generate more EMI. (7) Selection - Choose based on operating frequency. Fast recovery essential for SMPS above 50kHz.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use standard diodes for 50/60Hz; fast recovery for SMPS and high-frequency applications.

faqs.question: How do I parallel rectifier diodes for higher current?
faqs.answer: Paralleling diode guidelines: (1) Current sharing - Use small series resistors (0.01-0.1Ω) or ensure matched Vf. (2) Matching - Select diodes from same production batch. (3) Layout - Symmetrical layout with equal trace lengths. (4) Thermal - Common heatsink for temperature tracking. (5) Derating - Use 80% of combined rating. (6) Imbalance - Expect 10-20% current variation. (7) Testing - Verify sharing under load. With proper design, paralleled diodes can achieve 2-3x individual current.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use current sharing resistors or matched diodes; verify sharing under actual load conditions.

articles.title: Thermal Design for Power Semiconductors
articles.shortDescription: Comprehensive thermal design guidelines for IXYS power semiconductors
articles.content: Proper thermal design is essential for reliable operation of power semiconductors. This guide covers thermal resistance calculations, heatsink selection, thermal interface materials, and cooling strategies for IXYS MOSFETs, IGBTs, and thyristors.

sections.heading: Understanding Thermal Resistance
sections.content: Thermal resistance represents the temperature rise per unit of power dissipation. The total thermal resistance from junction to ambient consists of junction-to-case (Rth(j-c)), case-to-heatsink (Rth(c-s)), and heatsink-to-ambient (Rth(s-a)) resistances in series. Understanding these components is essential for accurate thermal design and ensuring junction temperature remains within safe limits.

sections.heading: Power Loss Calculation
sections.content: Accurate power loss calculation is the foundation of thermal design. For MOSFETs, calculate conduction losses using Rds(on) and switching losses using switching energy and frequency. For IGBTs, calculate conduction losses using Vce(sat) and switching losses using Eon and Eoff. These losses vary with operating conditions and must be calculated for worst-case scenarios.

sections.heading: Heatsink Selection and Cooling Methods
sections.content: Heatsink selection involves calculating required thermal resistance based on maximum junction temperature, ambient temperature, and total power dissipation. Various cooling methods are available: natural convection for low power, forced air for medium power, and liquid cooling for high power density applications. Thermal interface materials significantly impact overall thermal performance.

faqs.question: What is the thermal resistance chain in a power device?
faqs.answer: Thermal resistance chain: (1) Rth(j-c) - Junction to case, fixed by device design. (2) Rth(c-s) - Case to heatsink, depends on interface material. (3) Rth(s-a) - Heatsink to ambient, depends on heatsink design and airflow. (4) Total: Rth(j-a) = Rth(j-c) + Rth(c-s) + Rth(s-a). (5) Temperature rise: ΔT = P × Rth. (6) Junction temp: Tj = Ta + ΔT. (7) Design goal: Keep Tj below maximum rating (typically 150°C or 175°C). Understanding this chain is essential for proper thermal design.
faqs.decisionGuide: Minimize each thermal resistance; Rth(s-a) is usually the dominant term for natural convection.

faqs.question: How do I select the right thermal interface material?
faqs.answer: Thermal interface material selection: (1) Thermal grease - Best performance (0.1-0.5°C·cm²/W), requires careful application. (2) Thermal pads - Easier assembly (0.5-3°C·cm²/W), consistent thickness. (3) Phase change - Good compromise (0.2-0.5°C·cm²/W), flows under pressure. (4) Graphite - Excellent performance, expensive. (5) Application - Grease for high power, pads for ease of use. (6) Thickness - Thinner is better for grease. (7) Long-term stability - Consider pump-out and dry-out. For TO-247 packages, thermal grease typically provides best performance.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use thermal grease for best performance; phase change pads for easier manufacturing.

faqs.question: What cooling methods are available for power devices?
faqs.answer: Cooling methods comparison: (1) Natural convection - Simple, silent, limited to <50W per device. (2) Forced air - Moderate cost, good for 50-500W. (3) Liquid cooling - Best performance, 500W+, compact. (4) Heat pipes - Excellent for concentrated heat spreading. (5) Vapor chamber - Uniform temperature distribution. (6) Selection criteria - Power density, ambient temperature, noise, cost. (7) Hybrid - Combine methods for specific needs. For industrial drives, forced air or liquid cooling are most common.
faqs.decisionGuide: Choose based on power density; forced air for most applications; liquid for high power density.

faqs.question: How do I calculate the required heatsink size?
faqs.answer: Heatsink calculation procedure: (1) Determine total power loss P from electrical calculations. (2) Set maximum Tj (typically 125-150°C) and ambient Ta. (3) Calculate allowed ΔT = Tjmax - Ta. (4) Calculate required Rth(j-a) = ΔT/P. (5) Subtract Rth(j-c) and Rth(c-s) to get required Rth(s-a). (6) Select heatsink with Rth(s-a) less than calculated value. (7) Include 20% safety margin. Example: P=100W, Tjmax=125°C, Ta=50°C, Rth(j-c)=0.65, Rth(c-s)=0.2 → Rth(s-a) < (75/100) - 0.65 - 0.2 = 0.4°C/W.
faqs.decisionGuide: Calculate required Rth(s-a); select heatsink with adequate margin at specified airflow.

faqs.question: What is the effect of altitude on thermal performance?
faqs.answer: Altitude effects on cooling: (1) Air density - Decreases approximately 1.2% per 100m above sea level. (2) Natural convection - Rth increases ~10% per 1000m altitude. (3) Forced air - Rth increases ~5% per 1000m altitude. (4) Liquid cooling - Minimal altitude effect. (5) Derating - May need to reduce current at high altitude. (6) Example: At 3000m, natural convection Rth increases ~30%. (7) Design consideration - Account for maximum operating altitude. For high-altitude applications, liquid cooling or increased airflow may be needed.
faqs.decisionGuide: Derate for altitude above 1000m; consider liquid cooling for high-altitude high-power applications.

faqs.question: How do I verify my thermal design?
faqs.answer: Thermal verification methods: (1) Temperature measurement - Thermocouples on case and heatsink. (2) Infrared camera - Visual temperature distribution map. (3) Built-in sensors - Use NTC or diode temperature sensors if available. (4) Thermal simulation - CFD or thermal modeling software. (5) Overload testing - Verify under worst-case conditions. (6) Long-term monitoring - Track temperature trends during operation. (7) Validation - Compare measured vs calculated temperatures. Good correlation validates the design methodology and ensures reliable operation.
faqs.decisionGuide: Use multiple verification methods; validate with actual measurements under operating conditions.

faqs.question: How do I get technical support for ixys products?
faqs.answer: You can contact our technical support team via email, phone, or through our website. Our FAEs are available to help with product selection, application questions, and troubleshooting.

faqs.question: Where can I find datasheets and technical documentation?
faqs.answer: All datasheets, application notes, and technical documentation are available on our website. You can also contact us for specific documents or additional information.

faqs.question: Do you offer evaluation kits?
faqs.answer: Yes, evaluation kits are available for most products. Contact our sales team to request evaluation kits for your project.
